metadata.title: Certificate Authority (Windows AD CS / PKI) Component

components.title: Certificate Authority (Windows AD CS / PKI)
components.description: Windows Server Active Directory Certificate Services (AD CS) providing the organisation's internal Public Key Infrastructure (PKI). A two-tier PKI hierarchy is deployed: an offline Root CA (kept powered off and in a physically secure location) issues certificates to a subordinate Issuing CA (online). The Issuing CA issues certificates for: user authentication (smart card / Windows Hello for Business), computer authentication (802.1X), TLS server certificates for internal services, S/MIME email signing, and code signing.

props.name: platform
props.value: Windows Server 2022 Active Directory Certificate Services (AD CS)

props.name: hierarchy
props.value: Two-tier (offline Root CA + online Issuing/Subordinate CA)

props.name: issued-cert-types
props.value: User auth, computer auth, TLS server, code signing, S/MIME

protocols.name: https
protocols.title: HTTPS — CA web enrolment interface (if enabled) and CRL/OCSP publication

protocols.name: ldap
protocols.title: LDAP — Certificate publication to Active Directory

protocols.name: ocsp
protocols.title: OCSP — Online Certificate Status Protocol (certificate revocation checking)

port-ranges.remarks: OCSP responder served over HTTP per RFC 5019 recommendation

protocols.name: crl-http
protocols.title: CRL Distribution Point — Certificate Revocation List download

port-ranges.remarks: CRL download via HTTP — CDP URL embedded in issued certificates

protocols.name: dcom-rpc
protocols.title: DCOM/RPC — Certificate Authority MMC and certutil remote management

protocols.name: ms-cert-enroll
protocols.title: DCOM Certificate Enrolment — Autoenrollment and manual enrolment

port-ranges.remarks: Dynamic RPC high ports for DCOM certificate enrolment

control-implementations.description: Control implementations for Certificate Authority (Windows AD CS / PKI).

implemented-requirements.description: The AD CS Issuing CA is hardened per Microsoft PKI design guidance: the Root CA is kept offline and powered off between certificate issuance events; the Issuing CA private key is protected by a Hardware Security Module (HSM) where the data classification requires it; CA certificate templates are reviewed quarterly and templates not in use are disabled; the default CA templates are disabled and custom templates with appropriate security descriptors are used; audit logging is enabled on the CA for certificate issuance, revocation, and configuration changes.

CA audit events (certificate issuance, revocation, template changes, CA configuration changes, administrator access) are forwarded to the SIEM. Unexpected certificate issuance (e.g. code signing certificates issued outside of normal business hours) triggers security alerts.

CA private keys are protected using an HSM (Hardware Security Module) for the Root CA and Issuing CA. CA private keys cannot be exported. Where an HSM is not in scope, CA private keys are stored using Windows DPAPI with strong protection and are backed up to encrypted offline media stored in a physically secure location.
